nativeRange.habitat: Surge zone of clear seaward reef flats; benthopelagic, typically at depths of roughly 0–10 m where it schools over reef platforms exposed to wave action and strong currents

decorPreferences: large amounts of live rock for grazing

diet.notes: Primarily a grazer on filamentous and calcareous algae in the wild. In aquaria, dried marine seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based prepared foods are generally advised at least three times per week to support immunity and reduce aggression. Has been documented accepting frozen mysis, brine shrimp, and other meaty foods alongside tankmates, but algae should remain the dietary staple. Adequate algae availability is commonly reported to reduce aggression and lower the risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured fish in undersized systems

summary: An Indo-Pacific surgeonfish from shallow reef surge zones, distinguished by a grey body dotted with white spots, two silver bars on the forebody, and yellow tail and pelvic fins. Generally recommended for intermediate keepers with large, high-flow systems, and tends to be aggressive toward other tangs and similarly shaped fish.
careNotes:
The Mustard Guttatus Tang (Acanthurus guttatus), also widely known as the Whitespotted Surgeonfish, is a mid-sized Indo-Pacific surgeonfish that inhabits the surge zones of clear, exposed seaward reef flats. In the wild it forms schools over shallow reef platforms at depths of around 0–10 m, grazing on filamentous and calcareous algae. Adults reach around 22–30 cm in captivity.

A system of at least roughly 700 L (around 180 US gallons) with a long footprint and abundant open swimming space is generally advised, with experienced keepers commonly recommending substantially larger systems for adults. Strong flow and high oxygenation help replicate the surge-zone environment this species is accustomed to. Stable reef conditions — around 24–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, specific gravity 1.021–1.025, and alkalinity of 8–12 dKH — are commonly suggested.

Diet should be predominantly algae-based. Dried marine seaweed (nori) on a clip, spirulina-rich prepared foods, and grazable algae on live rock are generally considered the dietary staple; meaty foods such as mysis can be offered as supplements. Like other tangs, this species has been reported as susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and lateral stripe) when water quality slips or the diet is protein-heavy. Quarantine is generally advised before introduction, as this species has a documented susceptibility to ich and other skin parasites.

The Mustard Guttatus Tang tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other surgeonfishes but has been reported as peaceable toward most non-tang reef fish. Keeping only one specimen per system is generally advised, and introducing it last — after smaller and more timid tankmates have had time to settle in — is a commonly recommended practice. Like all surgeonfish, it carries a sharp spine at the base of the tail (the caudal peduncle) that is used in defence and is classified as venomous in this genus; handlers should use caution during net transfers.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Mustard Guttatus Tang has not been documented in aquarium-hobby literature. In the wild the species is presumed to spawn pelagically — releasing eggs and sperm into the open water column — like other Acanthurus species, but reproduction in home aquaria is considered effectively unattainable given the tank volumes and the pelagic larval rearing the young require.

primary.notes: Species product page for the Mustard Guttatus Tang. Provided adult size (12 in), minimum tank size (250 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.021–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Moderate), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs and surgeonfish, peaceful with others), reef compatibility (with caution), herbivorous diet, and the recommendation for live rock and marine seaweed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), adult size (26 cm TL max), native range (Indo-Pacific from East African oceanic islands to Hawaiian, Marquesan and Tuamoto islands; north to Ryukyu, south to New Caledonia and Rapa), depth (0–10 m), temperature (25–29.3°C, mean 28.2°C), surge-zone reef-flat habitat, herbivorous diet on filamentous and calcareous algae, schooling behaviour, and the genus' sharp forward-pointing caudal spine (classified as venomous in Acanthurus).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (nearly 10 in), minimum tank size (180 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH), moderate care level, semi-aggressive temperament toward similarly shaped tangs, reef-compatible status with caveat about picking at sessile inverts when underfed, and varied diet leaning algae-based.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Hobbyist species spotlight confirming surge-zone habitat, schooling behaviour over shallow reef platforms, and the distinctive white-spot / silver-bar / yellow-tail coloration of Acanthurus guttatus.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records are documented in available aquarium-hobby literature. Wild reproduction is presumed pelagic, similar to other Acanthurus species. Home-aquarium reproduction is considered effectively unattainable.
